text: The assistant introduces a metallic malleable retractor into the vagina to mobilize the uterus and expose the anterior vaginal wall. The surgeon gently dissects the vesico-vaginal septum using a swab, stopping just above the bladder neck. A shaped biological mesh is placed along the prepared vaginal wall, with its tip fixed at the deepest point. Four non-absorbable sutures are placed on the vaginal wall, with the final suture fixed near the anterior cervix.

The surgeon passes two polypropylene strings through the side wall of the abdomen in the preperitoneal space, outsourcing them three centimeters above the anterior and superior iliac spine. The ends of the strings are fixed with non-absorbable stitches to the apical portion of the biological mesh. The vaginal retractor is removed, and the peritoneum is sutured. The polypropylene tails are visible outside the skin, affixed to the abdominal external oblique muscle.

The surgeon incises the Denonvilliers fascia and opens the rectovaginal septum, without performing rectal mobilization or lateral dissection. A stitch is placed upon the sacral promontory, and a strip of biological mesh is inserted and sutured to the ventral aspect of the distal rectum, then further fixed to the lateral seromuscular borders of the proximal rectum.

The surgeon fixes the proximal part of the mesh to the sacral promontory, ensuring no traction is applied to the rectum. The surgical field shows the mesh securely attached without visible complications.
